{{Quote|Assisted Jedi Master Tra's M'ins in mediation of Duinuogwuin–Gotal conflict 1\68 to 4\66 PE.|Galactic Senate records on Jorus C'baoth|<ref name="Dark Force Rising" />}}
The '''Duinuogwuin–Gotal conflict''' was a short yet devastating war between the [[Duinuogwuin/Legends|Duinuogwuins]] and [[Gotal/Legends|Gotals]], two otherwise friendly [[Species/Legends|species]], in the last [[Standard decade/Legends|decades]] of the [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Galactic Republic]]. Rumor suggested that the sensitivity of the Gotal head cones had given rise to the clash through misguided interpretation of the Duinuogwuins' feelings. The conflict was settled in or at some point before [[27 BBY/Legends|27 BBY]] thanks to the intervention of [[Tra's M'ins]] and [[Jorus C'baoth]], two [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]] [[Diplomat/Legends|diplomats]] mandated by the Republic.

==The conflict==
[[File:Gotal_GOI.png|thumb|left|250px|The conflict may have started because of the sensitivity of the Gotals' head cones.]]
The Duinuogwuin–Gotal conflict was a two-[[Standard year/Legends|year]]-long war<ref name="Dark Force Rising" /> that was brought to an end in or at some point before [[27 BBY/Legends|27 BBY]].<ref name="Date" /> The conflict was between the [[Gotal/Legends|Gotals]], horned [[Humanoid/Legends|humanoids]] native to the [[Moon/Legends|moon]] [[Antar 4/Legends|Antar 4]], and the [[Duinuogwuin/Legends|Duinuogwuins]], a [[Species/Legends|species]] of winged, serpentine beings. The latter were normally reputed for being friendly and gregarious with strangers,<ref name="Galaxy Guide 4">''[[Galaxy Guide 4: Alien Races]]''</ref> and the Gotals were usually diplomatic and polite when interacting with members of other species.<ref name="Dark Force Rising Sourcebook">''[[Dark Force Rising Sourcebook]]''</ref>

Some speculated that the conflict between the two otherwise peaceful species was related to the sensitivity of the Gotal head cones. In fact, the Gotals used their nerve–filled horns to sense their environment and also to interpret the emotions of each other.<ref name="Dark Force Rising Sourcebook" /> According to old myths, their sensory horns could even detect [[Dark side of the Force/Legends|evil]].<ref name="KOTOR 40">[[Knights of the Old Republic 40|''Knights of the Old Republic'' 40]]</ref> In truth, the Gotals still had a hard [[Time/Legends|time]] when dealing with the emotions of other races, often mistaking mere affection for love, envy for murderous intent, or mild anger for imminent violence,<ref name="Dark Force Rising Sourcebook" /> and such misinterpretation had led the Gotals into clashes with other cultures.<ref name="DBgot">{{DB|species|gotal|Gotal}}</ref>

Although brief, the conflict was devastating. Eventually, the [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Galactic Republic]] sent the [[Jedi Master/Legends|Jedi Masters]] [[Jorus C'baoth]] and [[Tra's M'ins]] to mediate a peace. It was C'baoth, an experienced [[Diplomat/Legends|diplomat]] who had notably taken part in the [[Ando Demilitarization Observation Group|demobilization]] of the planet [[Ando/Legends|Ando]], who arranged an end to the hostilities<ref name="Dark Force Rising" /> before they grew into a full-scale war.<ref name="Dark Force Rising Sourcebook" /> However, even following the resolution of the conflict, the difficulty that Gotals experienced in interpreting the emotions of others became the source of new frictions. Indeed, just prior to the [[First Battle of Geonosis/Legends|start]] of the [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]]<ref name="CSWEGot">{{CSWECite|I|355|Gotal}}</ref> in [[22 BBY/Legends|22 BBY]],<ref name="NEC">''[[The New Essential Chronology]]''</ref> a group of Gotal extremists<ref name="CSWEGot" /> known as the [[Roshu Sune/Legends|Roshu Sune]]<ref name="HNN53">{{HoloNetNewsWeb|53|jediwatch/13425_1.html|Gotal Standoff Ends Violently}}</ref> engaged in a [[Battle of Antar 4 (Separatist Crisis)|bloody clash]] with the [[Jedi Order/Legends|Jedi Order]].<ref name="CSWEGot" />

The [[Galactic Senate/Legends|Galactic Senate]] of the Republic maintained records on the event.<ref name="Dark Force Rising" /> In [[9 ABY/Legends|9 ABY]],<ref name="ERC">''[[The Essential Reader's Companion]]''</ref> [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]], the first of a [[New Jedi Order|new generation]] of [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]], read a brief mention of C'baoth's involvement in the conflict while searching the Senate's records on the late Jedi Master.<ref name="Dark Force Rising" />

==Behind the scenes==
{{Quote|One of the more subtle goofs in Heir is in the dating. […] Personally, I put it down to the chaos of information loss during the Empire, and sloppy work on the part of post–Empire historians.|Timothy Zahn|<ref name="HE20">''[[Heir to the Empire: The 20th Anniversary Edition]]''</ref>}}
The Duinuogwuin–Gotal conflict was first mentioned in the [[1992]] novel ''[[Dark Force Rising]]'', the second installment of [[Timothy Zahn]]'s [[Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy|''Thrawn Trilogy'']]. In that book, the conflict was said to have taken place between 68 to 66 "Pre–[[Galactic Empire/Legends|Empire]]."<ref name="Dark Force Rising" /> However, Zahn's original dating was based on when he believed the Clone Wars had occurred. By then, the definitive timeline of the Clone Wars had not been settled yet, and ''[[Star Wars]]'' authors were told that the conflict had taken place thirty-five years before [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'': Episode IV ''A New Hope'']]. The release of the [[prequel trilogy]] between [[1999]] and [[2005]] established that the Clone Wars ended only nineteen years before Episode IV. Therefore, all the pre–Empire dates featured in the ''Thrawn Trilogy'' were rendered erroneous.<ref name="HE20" /> In his more recent [[Outbound Flight (novel)|''Outbound Flight'']] novel, Zahn placed the [[Death/Legends|death]] of Jorus C'baoth in 27 [[Galactic Standard Calendar|BBY]],<ref name="OBF">[[Outbound Flight (novel)|''Outbound Flight'']]</ref> thereby providing an approximate date for the Duinuogwuin–Gotal conflict.<ref name="Date" /> In ''[[Heir to the Empire: The 20th Anniversary Edition]]'', Zahn expressed his theory that the incorrect dates used in the trilogy were the work of careless [[in-universe]] [[Historian/Legends|historians]].<ref name="HE20" />
